coredata.dc:title: The luminescence profile of carbon dots synthesized from α-cellulose under different acid hydrolysis conditions
coredata.dc:creator: Ng, Yann Huey
Chin, Suk Fun
Pang, Suh Cem
Ng, Sing Muk
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the study on the luminescence properties portrayed by carbon dots synthesized under different acid hydrolysis conditions. Sulphuric acid was employed to dehydrate and carbonize α-cellulose as starting precursor. We revealed that the emissions of carbon dots were strongly dependent on the reaction condition during the synthesis, which include hydrolysis time and temperature. Initial mild acid hydrolysis at 4 °C and followed by incubation at room temperature was found favourable to the production of carbon dots emitting green fluorescence, whereas higher temperatures of 50 and 70 °C would produce carbon dots emitting blue fluorescence. At fixed temperature of 50 °C, prolonged synthesis time could shift the emission of carbon dots from green to blue. The green emission was recorded to have a peak at 500 nm when excited at 450 nm; whereas the highest intensity for the blue emission was recorded at 456 nm when excited at 357 nm. Both as-synthesized carbon dots showed excitation-independent emission. The quantum yields for the green and blue carbon dots were evaluated to be 6.4% and 4.0% respectively. The carbon dots emitting blue emission was of better photostability as compared to the carbon dots emitting green fluorescence. Both types of carbon dots synthesized in this study showed significant pH-dependent trend with higher intensity at lower pH condition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Nanoparticles
Carbon
Hydrolysis
Optical property

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of CDs synthesized from α-cellulose via sulphuric acid hydrolysis approach at 50 °C under (a) visible light and (b) ultraviolet (UV) light.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDsGreen synthesized via acid hydrolysis approach by incubation at 4 °C for 10 min and subsequently at room temperature; (i) absorption spectrum, (ii) excitation spectrum at 450 nm, and (iii) emission spectrum at 500 nm. Inset shows that the emission spectra of CDsGreen are excitation-independent. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDsBlue synthesized by acid hydrolysis of α-cellulose at 70 °C for an hour; (i) absorption spectrum, (ii) excitation spectrum at 357 nm, and (iii) emission spectrum at 456 nm. Inset shows that the emission spectra of CDsBlue are excitation-independent.

(a) Luminescence intensity monitored at 500 nm for CDsGreen synthesized at (i) 4 °C, (ii) 24 °C, (iii) 50 °C and (iv) 70 °C, and (b) luminescence intensity monitored at 456 nm for CDsBlue synthesized at (i) 4 °C, (ii) 24 °C, (iii) 50 °C and (iv) 70 °C.
CDs synthesized by mixing the reactants at (a) 4 °C for 10 min incubation and subsequently at 24 °C, and (b) 24 °C without incubation at 4 °C for the initial 10 min.

Fluorescence intensity of (i) CDsBlue monitored at emission wavelengths of 456 nm and (i) CDsGreen monitored at emission wavelengths of 500 nm, which was intended to show the photostability effect for the CDs under continuous light excitation.
FTIR absorption spectra of (i) untreated α-cellulose, (ii) CDsGreen and (iii) CDsBlue.
Variation of fluorescence intensity of CDsBlue and CDsGreen in aqueous solutions of different pH values.
TEM image of CDsBlue synthesized from α-cellulose via acid dehydration method at 70 °C for 4 h.

head.ce:title: The luminescence profile of carbon dots synthesized from α-cellulose under different acid hydrolysis conditions

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Resource Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Engineering, Computing, and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia.(S.M. Ng)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering, Computing, and Science
Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the study on the luminescence properties portrayed by carbon dots synthesized under different acid hydrolysis conditions. Sulphuric acid was employed to dehydrate and carbonize α-cellulose as starting precursor. We revealed that the emissions of carbon dots were strongly dependent on the reaction condition during the synthesis, which include hydrolysis time and temperature. Initial mild acid hydrolysis at 4 °C and followed by incubation at room temperature was found favourable to the production of carbon dots emitting green fluorescence, whereas higher temperatures of 50 and 70 °C would produce carbon dots emitting blue fluorescence. At fixed temperature of 50 °C, prolonged synthesis time could shift the emission of carbon dots from green to blue. The green emission was recorded to have a peak at 500 nm when excited at 450 nm; whereas the highest intensity for the blue emission was recorded at 456 nm when excited at 357 nm. Both as-synthesized carbon dots showed excitation-independent emission. The quantum yields for the green and blue carbon dots were evaluated to be 6.4% and 4.0% respectively. The carbon dots emitting blue emission was of better photostability as compared to the carbon dots emitting green fluorescence. Both types of carbon dots synthesized in this study showed significant pH-dependent trend with higher intensity at lower pH condition.

ce:section.ce:para: The recent emerging of luminescence carbon dots (CDs) have attracted immense attention and wide utilisation for different applications due to their good biocompatibility, simple synthetic routes, tuneable surface functionalities, and most notably, as an alternative for quantum dots (QDs) that are mostly reported to be highly toxic [1]. CDs were serendipitously discovered by Xu and co-workers during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes about a decade ago [2]. Since then, various synthesis approaches have been successfully explored, which include laser ablation [3], electrochemical [4], thermal carbonisation [5] and acid dehydration [6]. At the same time, various starting precursors rich in carbon content were explored to be converted into CDs, which some of the examples are such as food wastes [7], hair fibres [8], bovine serum albumin [9] and even peels of fruits [10]. It then progresses towards utilising the CDs for useful applications and the most promising areas are in optical sensing and bio-imaging due to the unique luminescence properties [11,12]. It is also more favourable due to the cleaner nature from production to disposal that can promote towards better sustainable practise in science and technology [13].

In order to better suit CDs for various applications; it is crucial to explore and understand more on its luminescence nature and to have better control over factors that are affecting the luminescence property. Great attention has been focusing on unveiling the origin of the luminescence. Some possible mechanisms proposed include quantum confinement effect [14], emissive traps [15], free zig-zag sites [16] and surface state due to attached chemical groups, molecule state due to solely fluorescent molecules attached on the surface of or within the CDs, as well as crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect [17]. CDs synthesized from various synthetic pathways using diverse starting precursors exhibited different fluorescence properties lead to different luminescence mechanisms. Doping of foreign species is also reported to alter the luminescence property of CDs [18,19]. Despite much progress has been made, the origin of luminescence for CDs is still debatable. Direct comparison from different works reported in the literature is not possible due to the lack in experimental control in the synthesis condition and the reagents. Thus, systematic investigation on factors that affect the luminescence property of the CDs can provide a better insight of this class of nanomaterial. Jiang and co-workers have successfully synthesized red, green and blue CDs from three different phenylenediamine isomers [20]. Some reports have also shown tuneable luminescence profile from green to blue through surface modification or reduction process [21]. The work reported by Nie and co-workers has successfully produced excitation-independent CDs with blue fluorescence as well as excitation-dependent CDs with full-colour emissions by controlling the reaction time [22]. Despite all these reported works, continuous works in this area are still crucial to generate more new information to better understand the luminescence nature of CDs.

This study was planned to investigate the luminescence nature of the CDs treated with different acid hydrolysis conditions during the synthesis process. The main aim is to reveal the luminescence behaviour of the CDs during the synthesis; having the motivation to formulate an approach towards a tuneable luminescence of CDs using the synthesis parameters, while employing the same starting materials and reagents. This has not been well reported in the literature and worth investigation. The selected source for the starting material is α-cellulose, which is a common insoluble fibrous residue that can be found in agricultural waste such as wood, straw pulps and holocelluloses [23]. Substantial amount of agricultural biomass wastes containing high content of celluloses can be produced by agro-based countries and making it a clean and sustainable precursor for the synthesis of CDs. The synthesis adopted facile one-pot synthesis approach to minimize the number of synthesis variables and to reduce the step of synthesis. Concentrated sulphuric acid which served as a strong dehydrating and carbonisation agent was used to convert α-cellulose into CDs. Different hydrolysis conditions were controlled by changing the used incubation temperature and the sequent in applying the various chosen temperatures. The luminescence properties of the as-prepared CDs were explored and reported in this paper.

All chemicals used were of analytical grades unless otherwise stated. α-cellulose of in white powder form was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Sulphuric acid (98%) was purchased from RCI Labscan Chemicals and pure sodium hydroxide pellets was purchased from Hudson Chemicals. Quinine hemisulfate salt monohydrate was purchased from Sigma Aldrich while acetone was purchased from HmbG Chemicals. Ultrapure water obtained from Milipore Mili-Q Advantage-A10 and Milipore Elix-5 water purification system (∼18.2 mΩ, 25 °C) was used throughout the study. All reagents employed in this study were used as received without further purification unless otherwise stated.

The CDs were produced via carbonisation of organic precursor using strong acid hydrolysis process and the synthesized method was adopted from the work by Chin and co-workers with some modifications [24]. In the typical synthesis procedure, α-cellulose (0.04 g) was first dispersed in 1.0 ml ultrapure water. The dispersion was dripped drop wise into 3.0 ml of chilled concentrated sulphuric acid (98%). The mixture was then briefly swirled to obtain homogenous solution, followed by incubation at different temperatures ranging from 4 to 70 °C. The solutions incubated at different temperatures were monitored over a period of prefixed time. The colour of the solution has changed from colourless to brownish solutions and this gave the indication on the formation of CDs. The resultant CDs solutions were then diluted with ultrapure water and filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove large carbon residues for further study.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse Varian). CDs in aqueous medium were diluted with ultrapure water and transferred to a four-side clear quartz cuvette with a path length of 1.0 cm. The settings of excitation and emission wavelengths were altered accordingly for CDs synthesized at different temperature to obtain optimum fluorescence excitation and emission. The excitation and emission slits were fixed at 5 and 10 nm respectively throughout the study. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained using UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 50). The absorbance of samples was recorded within the range of 200–800 nm. Ultrapure water was used as reagent blank for the absorbance measurement. pH meter (Mettler Toledo SevenEasy) was used for adjustment of pH and water bath (Memmert WNB 14) was used to control the synthesis temperatures. Vacuum concentrator (Labconco Acid-Resistant CentriVap) was used to remove the excess solvent during isolation of CDs. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEOL Model JEM 1010) was used to characterize the CDs sample by drying approximately 5 μl of CDs sample on TEM copper grid prior to imaging. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) (Perkin-Elmer) was used to characterize the organic functional groups present on the surface of CDs.

The quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized carbon dots was calculated based on Equation (1) [8]:
(1)
Q X
=
Q
S T
(
m X
/
m
S T
)
(
n X 2
/
n
S T
2
)
where ST and X depicted the standard and sample, respectively; Q depicted the quantum yield, m depicted the gradient obtained from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance, and n depicted the refractive index of solvent used. Quinine sulphate dissolved in 0.1 M sulphuric acid (n = 1.33) was used as the reference standard (literature QST = 0.54) for QY determination of CDs [25].

In the initial stage of this study, the CDs isolated at the different stages of carbonisation of α-cellulose using concentrated sulphuric acid at 50 °C were found to emit two different fluorescence colours. The one isolated approximately after 1 h showed green emission while the one isolated after 5 h showed blue emission when exposed to the UV light as shown in Fig. 1 . This was a strong evidence that the α-cellulose has been converted into CDs since other materials involved in the synthesis have showed no or low fluorescence when checked using the spectrofluorometer. The two emission profiles also suggested that there were at least two major types of CDs isolated. Accordingly, the emission intensity can be correlated as semi-quantitative indicator for the CDs concentration, where higher intensity will reflect the presence of more CDs and vice versa.

When recorded using the spectrofluorometer, the CDs with green emission under the UV (CDsGreen) showed to exhibit an emission band with peak wavelength at 500 nm when excited at the optimum wavelength of 450 nm, whereas the CDs with blue emission (CDsBlue) showed a band with peak wavelength at 456 nm when excited at the optimum wavelength of 357 nm (Fig. 2 ). A small shoulder peak of the CDsBlue was suggested to be caused by either the transmission property of the CDs or the presence of trace impurities in the surrounding of the CDs as this is similarly observed in other works [26]. Both the as-prepared CDs have portrayed an excitation-independent behaviour, where fluorescence intensity varied but no shift in the emission peak when the excitation wavelength was changed from 350 to 460 nm at an interval of 10 nm (insert Fig. 2). This excitation-independent emission has suggested less types of surface defects on the CDs and the CDs were distributed more uniformly in sizes [27]. As for the absorption spectra (Fig. 2), both the sample showed strong absorption in the range of 300 nm and this can obviously due to the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 of the carbon [28,29]. As the spectra tailed into the visible range, the CDsGreen showed a broader band with a slight shoulder peaking at 340 nm due to the n-π* transitions [30], while this was not observed for the CDsBlue. This difference suggested that the CDsGreen were richer in the surface functional moieties that were able to trap the excited state energy as compared to the CDsBlue. This could possibly due to the incomplete carbonisation of α-cellulose under shorter hydrolysis time and the impurities were passivated at the surface of the nanostructure as defects.

Initial results have clearly showed that shorter time of carbonisation led to green emission while longer carbonisation tends to blue-shift the emission. This leads to the postulate that the degree of carbonisation of α-cellulose by the strong acid can produce CDs that emit different emissions. This can be due to the differences in morphology and surface defects for CDs that were produced via different conditions. There have been some reports suggested that the blue emission was due to the quantum effect caused by the core of the nanoparticles, while emission at the visible range was induced by the surface defects [31,32]. This matches well the observation of the luminescence property observed in this study. In this case, shorter hydrolysis time can lead to incomplete carbonisation, leaving residue of simple molecules from α-cellulose to be passivated on the nanostructure surface as electron traps. Longer hydrolysis can cause more complete carbonisation with less passivation effect, leading to the formation of cleaner bare CDs. There is also study showed that larger CDs can shift the emission towards the visible range and this has suggested that the CDsGreen were larger in size compared to the CDsBlue due to the surface functional groups [33]. There is a similar study showed that harsh carbonisation via pyrolysis has produced CDs emitting blue fluorescence, while mild carbonisation via hydrothermal process has produced CDs emitting green fluorescence, where both approaches used the same starting precursor [34]. However, the study used two different methods as compared to this work that adopted only one method, but instead controlling the hydrolysis condition. The use of acid with less hydrolysis strength can also shift the emission towards longer wavelength as due to mild carbonisation process [35].

In order to investigate further on the postulate, the degree of carbonisation by the acid was controlled using temperature. It is well-known that lower temperature leads to slower hydrolysis while higher temperature can promote the kinetic energy of a system for rapid and vigorous hydrolysis process, thus better carbonisation. In this study, a mixture consisting of α-cellulose and concentrated sulphuric acid was divided into 4 identical portions and each was incubated separately in well controlled temperature of 4, 24, 50 and 70 °C. As proposed, the temperature applied during the synthesis was found to show significant effect on the formation of CDs and eventually led to the production of CDs with different emissions. Generally, lower temperature during synthesis was found to promote formation of CDsGreen while higher temperature favoured the formation of CDsBlue (Fig. 3 ). For instance, sample synthesized at 4 °C showed a gradual increase of fluorescence at 500 nm over time, but no fluorescence intensity was recorded at 456 nm, indicating the presence of only CDsGreen. When the synthesis temperature was set at 70 °C, the blue emission at 456 nm was observed to be high even just after 1 h of synthesis, while low emission at 500 nm was recorded for the same sample. These results strongly suggested that the temperature has altered the carbonisation kinetics, and led to the formation of different kinds of CDs with unique emission spectra profiles. Generally, mild temperatures flavour the formation of CDsGreen, and high temperatures flavour towards CDsBlue.

Hsu and Chang (2012) have proposed a four-stage mechanism for the formation of CDs, which involved dehydration of precursors, polymerization of intermediates or aromatisation, nucleation, and crosslinking that promotes particles growth [36]. In this study, α-cellulose was first dehydrated by sulphuric acid to form intermediates that further proceeded with polymerization due to the reactivity of these intermediate units. Concentrated sulphuric acid was chosen due to its strong hydrolysis strength that is required to carbonize the strong polymeric structure of α-cellulose used in this study. Unlike simple monomers or oligomers where milder acid such as phosphoric acid can be utilised for the carbonisation process to form CDs [37]. Under low temperature, the dehydration and polymerization processes were expected to be slower and would not favour the next nucleation process to form the CDs. The intermediates instead crosslink with the existing nuclei and eventually promoted the growth of the particles. This is in agreement with the fluorescence profile of CDs synthesized at low temperature which shifted emission spectrum at the longer wavelength region. CDs with growing sizes were reported to show a red-shift in the fluorescence emission [33]. As the temperature during synthesis process increased, the active hydrolysis could rapidly produce intermediates at higher concentration and promoted active nucleation to occur. Eventually, this can lead to the formation of smaller particles and shift the emission towards the blue region.

To further validate the suggested explanations on the observed luminescence trend, a test was performed by mixing the reactants at lower temperature of around 4 °C and maintained at that temperature for 10 min before warming the mixture to 24 °C. The intention to start at lower temperature was to allow slower rate of hydrolysis to occur during the initial stage that can reduce rapid nucleation. After the nucleation stage, temperature was increased to promote particles growth on the exiting nucleus in forming the CDs. The results obtained clearly showed that such approach had promoted the final intensity of CDsGreen by at least 25% after 4 h as compared to the batch of sample that was synthesized at fixed temperature of 24 °C (Fig. 4 ). There was no fluorescence recorded within the blue range at this stage and the synthesis condition was still considered mild. However, it is worth noting that when CDsGreen was exposed to higher temperature of 70 °C, the green emission at 500 nm started to decrease gradually and the blue emission showed an increasing trend. This means that the hydrolysis continued on the CDsGreen with further strong carbonisation that can destroy the surface defects, and eventually reducing the average size of the nanostructure. The reduction in surface defects and size will change the luminescence property as both factors are often suggested as the origin of the emission for CDs [17].

CDs are generally known to have high photostability with potential applications in various fields. In order to determine the photostability at room temperature of the as-prepared CDsGreen and CDsBlue, both the samples were exposed with UV light at their respective optimum excitation wavelengths for 75 min. The intensity of the fluorescence for both the CDs were measured every 5-min interval at 500 and 456 nm respectively. As clearly shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence emission of CDsGreen was found to have decreased by 23.22% over 75 min. This result suggested that the as-prepared CDsGreen were less stable. At room temperature, the fluorescence of CDsGreen was slowly shifted towards the blue region which was similar to the trend observed for samples that were synthesized at high temperature. This leads to the suggestion that CDsGreen could eventually be converted into CDsBlue upon excitation at ambient condition. The fluorescence emission of CDsBlue showed a decrease of an approximate 1.86% only from its initial intensity under continuous excitation. This indicates that CDsBlue possessed higher photostability when compared to CDsGreen and hence more suitable for practical applications. The acid has converted majority of the staring precursor into the final product of CDsBlue. The decreased in fluorescence intensities could be a result of photobleaching, a photo-induced chemical destruction process that eventually leads to fluorescence decay of fluorophores upon continuous exposure to UV irradiation [38].

The relative QY of both CDsGreen and CDsBlue were also determined by comparison to quinine sulphate standard, with its literature represented QY value of 0.54. The QY values of CDsGreen and CDsBlue were determined to be 0.064 and 0.040 respectively. These QY values are comparable with most of CDs systems reported in the literature [39,40]. Furthermore, FTIR was used to characterize the organic functional groups that are present on the surface of CDs. The FTIR spectrum of the untreated α-cellulose, CDsGreen and CDsBlue are shown in Fig. 6 , respectively. The stretching vibration of OH, CH, CO and COC could be found in untreated α-cellulose at 3329, 2888 cm, 1158 and 1025 cm−1, respectively. Similarly, these vibration stretches were also found present in both CDsGreen and CDsBlue, suggesting the presence of alcohol or phenol, alkyl and carboxylic acid on the CDs surface. Most of the signature peaks for α-cellulose such as the peaks at 2888 and 1641 cm−1 were preserved strongly in the spectrum for CDsGreen, supporting the suggestion that the presence of α-cellulose residues in it due to incomplete carbonisation. Whereas the peaks for CDsBlue were sharp with less shoulder, indicating less type of functional groups present in the CDsBlue sample. Besides, the FTIR spectrum of CDsBlue also clearly showed a more distinct stretching vibration of CH2 at 1399 cm−1 compared to the untreated α-cellulose and CDsGreen. This has indicated that CDsBlue had undergone higher carbonisation degree compared to CDsGreen, taking the spectrum of untreated α-cellulose as reference.

Since the synthesis of CDs has adopted strong acid as carbonisation agent, it is of high possibility that the surface of the CDs synthesized in this study will be oxidised by the sulphuric acid. Commonly, functional groups such as the carboxylic acid and hydroxyl have been reported to form at the surface of CDs after treatment with acid [41]. Furthermore, the good solubility of the isolated CDs has further support the presence of these functional groups, as bare CDs consisting of majority carbon will not be too soluble in water. Thus, protonation and deprotonation should take place in the change of the surrounding pH. In this study, the pH of the solution has found to greatly affecting the luminescence intensity of both the formed CDs under different conditions as predicted (Fig. 7 ). Since the initial solution pH of samples was strongly acidic, sodium hydroxide was used to adjust the pH of the solutions. In order to standardize the condition and to minimize the effect of dilution for accurate comparison between the samples, water was added to each of the sample after the addition of sodium hydroxide to a fixed total volume of 3.7 mL. Addition of water would also reduce the concentration of salt formed during the neutralization process and can minimize the possible interference effect on the luminescence profile. The resulting pH value and the fluorescence intensity were recorded.

Both types of CDs showed strong fluorescence under acidic condition at pH < 3 and showed no or low fluorescence above pH 12. The CDsGreen showed a very narrow pH-dependent range with no fluorescence observed as pH values above 3. As it was suggested by the previous results, CDsGreen have more functional groups on the surface and thus can interact effectively with the changing of pH condition. Such interaction can modify the state of electron defects on the surface and eventually destroy the initial luminescence process since the green emission was proposed to be originated from the surface defects. Besides, the gradual deprotonation due to higher pH condition could have induced aggregation that will also decrease significantly the luminescence of CDsGreen. It was observed that the recovery of luminescence was not possible even when the pH value was readjusted to below 3.

As for the CDsBlue, the pH dependent range was wider, with gradual decrease in luminescence intensity as the pH value was increased. The CDsBlue were also observed to be more stable with reversible fluorescence observed under changing pH condition. Our findings concurred with results reported recently by Wang and co-workers that the luminescence showed decreasing intensity from its maximum at pH 3 to the basic pH range [42]. The increasing pH value could lead to increasing the negative charges on the surface due to deprotonation, which could attract ionic impurities such as counter ions and form complexes. The close proximity often promotes non-radiative pathway for the relaxation of an excited species and this is as reflected in the drop of luminescence intensity observed in this study. However, the degree of quenching was less drastic compared to the CDsGreen, as the luminescence origin proposed was not from surface defects.

The CDsBlue have been successfully isolated for TEM imaging using salting out approach reported previously [43]. Crystallization of sodium sulphate salt formed from neutralization reaction between sulphuric acid and sodium hydroxide can be promoted in the presence of acetone. After neutralization of the CDsBlue solution to pH 7, equal volume of acetone was added to promote crystallization of the sodium sulphate salts. The solution was mixed vigorously prior to incubation at 4 °C to promote salt nucleation and crystal growth. The CDsBlue have partitioned in the acetone as evidence from the change of colour for colourless to yellow. The CDsBlue has been easily isolated from the acetone using vacuum concentrator. The CDsBlue were then concentrated by lyophilization to remove excess water. The yield of CDsBlue from α-cellulose was calculated to be 29.58% w/w. The TEM image of CDsBlue is as shown in Fig. 8 . There was no significant change in the morphology observed from different batches of CDsBlue obtained under different hydrolysis routes.

It was found that the CDsBlue were very homogenously distributed, having spherical shapes with the diameter in the range of 10–20 nm. In this case, we postulated that the formation of the CDsBlue was via the re-polymerization process from the active intermediates during the hydrolysis process. The intermediates were more concentrated in carbon content as hydrolysis process has eliminated some other elements via the production of side products such as water molecules. In the case of producing CDsBlue, rapid hydrolysis has produced high concentration of intermediates at a time and referring to the Relative Supersaturation (RSS) principle, this high concentration will lead to active nucleation. The nucleus will start to grow evenly and forming homogenous sphere nanoparticles when the concentration of the intermediates has started to decrease as there was lesser starting precursors available after the continuous hydrolysis process.

In the case of CDsGreen, the isolation of the sample for TEM imaging was found challenging. The neutralization has caused the sample to aggregate where no clear image of nanoparticles can be obtained under the TEM. Direct sample preparation on the copper grid without neutralization was not performed due to the extreme acidic condition that was corrosive, while it was difficult to evaporate away the acid for imaging. Attempt to isolate the sample is still on-going.

We have successfully demonstrated the synthesis of CDs with different luminescence properties by precisely controlling the reaction time and temperature during the carbonisation of α-cellulose via acid hydrolysis approach. Mild hydrolysis at lower temperatures (below 25 °C) during the initial synthesis stage was found crucial to promote the formation of CDs emitting green emission. On the contrary, higher incubation temperatures at 50–70 °C in water bath for a prolonged time can result in the formation of CDs emitting blue emission. These luminescence trends and the CDs characteristics have suggested that mild hydrolysis leads to less vigorous carbonisation of α-cellulose, promoting higher possibility of surface passivation by the by-products of the hydrolysis. This will increase the size of the CDs besides creating surface defects that flavoured towards producing emission in the visible region. As for harsh hydrolysis condition, complete carbonisation will occur and producing smaller and cleaner CDs that flavour towards blue emission. This is postulated to be caused by the quantum confinement effects from the small carbon core.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) provided by Ministry of Education Malaysia (Grant No. FRGS/2/2013/SG06/SWIN/03/1). The authors would also like to acknowledge the support by all staffs, technicians and postgraduates of Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus and Universiti Malaysia Sarawak (UNIMAS) who have contributed to this study.
